text:
There is something the rain does to time — it thickens it, the way cream thickens water, slowing the afternoon into a substance you can almost hold. The light goes pewter and diffuse, stripped of its usual ambitions, no longer trying to cast shadows or declare directions, and in that gentled, sourceless illumination the familiar room becomes a kind of philosophical proposition about itself, every object quietly insisting on its own weight and presence.

Sound, too, undergoes a transformation. The percussion on the window is not noise exactly but rather a form of white privacy, a curtain drawn not across space but across the social world, the world of errands and appointments and the tyranny of the possible. Inside the rain's parentheses, you are released from forward motion. The afternoon achieves a kind of moral permission to be unproductive, and this permission is deeply, almost scandalously pleasurable.

What the rainy afternoon aestheticizes, finally, is interiority itself. It makes a theater of the inner life. Steam rises from a mug with almost aggressive coziness; the pages of whatever you are reading seem slightly more absorbent, more willing to receive attention. A peculiar tenderness accrues to things: the cat arranged in a comma of sleep, the window's trembling calligraphy of water, the smell of wet earth transporting you to every other wet afternoon you have ever inhabited, a sudden superimposition of all the rainy rooms you have ever sat in, each one nested inside this one like a hall of silver mirrors.

The Japanese have a word, aware, that gesture toward the bittersweetness of passing things. The rainy afternoon is essentially aware — beautiful precisely because it will lift, because the temporal looseness is borrowed, because the pewter light is already tilting toward evening, and you know it, and that knowing is half the pleasure.
